title: RPYR-0016 — `classmethod` declares no GC `traverse` while its sibling `staticmethod` does, so a reference cycle through a classmethod's wrapped callable is uncollectable (builtins/classmethod.rs:30)
short_title: `classmethod` missing GC traverse → cycle leak (staticmethod has it)
one_line_repro:
def f(cls): pass
f.ref=n; cm=classmethod(f); n.cm=cm   # cycle n->cm->f->n leaks (CPython collects)
prior_art: Same class as RPYR-0010 (deque/defaultdict) and RPYR-0012 (itertools.cycle): a ref-owning #[pyclass] with no `traverse`. Follows the merged traverse rollout PR #6780 ("traverse and clear") which added traverse across list/dict/tuple/generator/frame but skipped classmethod. No specific RustPython issue. Distinct from RUSTPY-0009/0011 (staticmethod/classmethod *repr* .unwrap()).
found_by: git-history-analyzer (informed explore — spotted the smoking-gun asymmetry: sibling staticmethod.rs:10 has #[pyclass(traverse)] over an identical field; classmethod never got it)
gc-traverse-auditor (listed classmethod among the ~54 missing-traverse payloads)
cpython_behavior: collects — CPython's classmethod defines tp_traverse over cm_callable, so a cycle through the wrapped callable is reclaimed by gc.collect().
guarded_twin: The ADJACENT sibling `PyStaticMethod` (staticmethod.rs:10) is declared `#[pyclass(... traverse)]` over an identical `callable: PyMutex<PyObjectRef>` field. Verified live in one harness: the staticmethod cycle collects, the classmethod cycle leaks. Strongest possible static signal — the correct twin is one type over.
affected: classmethod (a cycle through cm.__func__ / the wrapped callable)
notes: `PyClassMethod` owns `callable: PyMutex<PyObjectRef>` but its `#[pyclass]` declares no `traverse`, so HAS_TRAVERSE=false and the collector never traces the callable — a cycle routed through a classmethod's wrapped function is uncollectable. Weakref-proven: `def f(cls): pass; f.ref=n; cm=classmethod(f); n.cm=cm` makes the cycle n->cm->f->n; after `del` + `gc.collect()` the node is alive on RustPython, collected on CPython; a user-class control collects on both. The identical staticmethod cycle collects on RustPython because staticmethod.rs:10 carries `traverse`. FIX: add `traverse` to classmethod's #[pyclass] (derive traces `callable`), exactly the #6780 pattern. Broader: this is one of ~54 ref-owning payloads still missing traverse (gc-traverse-auditor), incl. the deque/dict/set iterators and the itertools accumulate/groupby/tee/pairwise accumulators — see [[RPYR-0010]] and [[RPYR-0012]].
